current_fact.fact_text: On March 31, 1901, Antonín Dvořák’s opera "Rusalka" premiered in Prague, telling the eerie, romantic tale of a water nymph who gives up her voice and nature to seek human love. Its haunting "Song to the Moon" would become one of the most beloved arias in the operatic repertoire.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

current_fact.match_reason: Rusalka is a tragic study in irrational, self-sacrificing love; an episode devoted to love’s vulnerable edge and its artistic power is a perfect modern echo of Dvořák’s moonlit opera.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1913, a Vienna concert of modernist music led by Arnold Schoenberg descended into chaos as parts of the audience rioted, outraged by the radical sounds of atonality. The "Skandalkonzert" ended early, with insults, fistfights, and a police intervention becoming as legendary as the music itself.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Schoenberg’s scandalous concert and the birth of trip-hop both represent moments when new sonic languages disrupted expectations and split audiences, turning experimentation into a cultural fault line.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1521, Ferdinand Magellan and about fifty of his men came ashore at Limasawa in the Philippines to attend what is considered the first Catholic Mass in the archipelago. It was both a religious ritual and a symbolic claim, as imperial expansion and faith traveled together across oceans.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: Magellan’s voyage to distant islands and the sea-bound Mass at Limasawa align with an episode framed as a boat journey across seas and cultures, both exploring how belief and identity travel over water.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1717, Bishop Benjamin Hoadly delivered a sermon in London that questioned the authority of the church, sparking a fierce theological and political dispute known as the Bangorian Controversy. His claim that Christ’s kingdom was not of this world challenged long-held assumptions about power, faith, and who gets to speak for the divine.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: Hoadly’s sermon literally forced his society to 'think again' about religious authority, making it a historical parallel to an episode centered on questioning inherited beliefs and unlearning.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1942, Japanese forces invaded Christmas Island in the Indian Ocean, capturing a remote British possession that was rich in phosphate deposits. The tiny island suddenly found itself pulled into the vast, global machinery of World War II.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: A remote tropical island suddenly entangled in empire and war echoes through dub and roots reggae’s long conversation with colonial history, "Babylon," and distant islands pulled into global struggle.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 307, Constantine—future emperor Constantine the Great—married Fausta, daughter of the retired emperor Maximian, after divorcing his earlier partner Minervina. The marriage was a calculated political alliance that helped secure his claim to imperial power in a fractured Roman world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

queue.match_reason: Constantine’s world of emperors, ritual, and religious symbolism parallels Ghost’s theatrical play with clergy, power, and shifting identities—both stories of how authority is staged and embodied.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1992, the USS Missouri, the last active battleship in the United States Navy, was officially decommissioned in Long Beach, California. The "Mighty Mo" had seen combat from World War II to the Gulf War, and its retirement marked the end of the era of big-gun battleships.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: The retirement of a symbol of heavy, state-backed firepower aligns with a band that spent its career critiquing war, empire, and militarized power structures in explosive sonic form.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1995, Tejano superstar Selena Quintanilla-Pérez was murdered in a motel in Corpus Christi, Texas by the president of her own fan club. Her death at just 23 froze a rising career that was on the verge of breaking into global pop stardom and left a lasting cultural scar.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: Selena’s life and loss fit within a tribute to powerful women’s voices; the episode’s focus on soul-bearing, unapologetic femininity honors the kind of presence she embodied on and off stage.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 2016, NASA’s "Year in Space" mission not only tested human endurance but also produced one of the most unusual twin studies in science: Scott Kelly in orbit and his identical brother Mark Kelly on Earth. Researchers compared their bodies down to the level of genes, watching how spaceflight literally rewrote some of Scott’s biology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A long, meticulously studied mission that literally rewired a human body in space echoes an episode about how sustained effort and learning reshape the brain over time.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 2016, the same mission that tested human limits in orbit also turned the International Space Station into a kind of cultural outpost, with Scott Kelly’s photos and messages bringing Earth’s curvature and auroras into millions of timelines. The ISS became a place where science, art, and public imagination met in real time.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: As the ISS mission turned a technical lab into a shared global experience, this first Sedna FM episode marks the moment a small project launches 'into the universe' of listeners—both are origin stories of a new kind of broadcast from above.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1717, the Bangorian Controversy didn’t just divide theologians; it spilled into pamphlets, coffeehouse debates, and public opinion, showing how ideas about authority and conscience could ripple through everyday life. It was an early reminder that even abstract arguments about faith are also arguments about how people get to live.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: A fierce public dispute about who can define belief and conscience connects well with an episode dedicated to breaking out of boxes and rejecting imposed labels and authorities.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1942, the invasion of tiny Christmas Island underscored how World War II reached even the most remote corners of the globe. An island named for a holiday became a contested speck on the map, reminding us that global conflicts redraw the meaning of places we once thought were far away from everything.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A remote ocean island drawn into war pairs with an episode that dives into another isolated ocean frontier, shifting the focus from conflict to awe in the world’s far-flung waters.

published.fact_text: In 2016, NASA astronaut Scott Kelly and Roscosmos cosmonaut Mikhail Kornienko returned to Earth after spending nearly a full year aboard the International Space Station, one of the longest continuous spaceflights ever completed. Their mission was designed as a living experiment to understand how long-term space travel changes the human body and mind, paving the way for future journeys to Mars.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal year in orbit pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space, echoing the psychological and physical 'space shift' experienced on the ISS.

published.fact_text: In 1966, the Soviet Union launched Luna 10, which soon became the first artificial satellite to orbit the Moon. Hidden inside was a tiny transmitter that played the Internationale during the 23rd Congress of the Communist Party, turning a scientific milestone into a piece of cosmic theatre.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: Luna 10 turned orbital mechanics into a political performance, much like an Evening Flow turns a sequence of sounds into a drifting, narrative journey through space and mood.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 2005, astronomers at Palomar Observatory discovered Makemake, a distant dwarf planet in the Kuiper belt. Its bright, icy surface and lonely orbit beyond Neptune helped reshape our definition of what counts as a planet in our solar system.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A newly found dwarf planet in the outer solar system pairs with a 'Cosmic Monday' built on futuristic, spacey disco, turning orbital discovery into a danceable transmission.

published.fact_text: Explorer 1, the very first satellite launched by the United States, re-entered Earth’s atmosphere on March 31, 1970 after more than 58,000 trips around the planet. During its early life it helped reveal the Van Allen radiation belts, showing that Earth is wrapped in invisible, charged rings of space weather.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: Explorer 1’s long orbital voyage and its role in mapping Earth’s invisible environment resonate with an episode explicitly framed as a multi-stop 'Destination' journey through different musical territories.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1761, a massive earthquake with an estimated magnitude of 8.5 struck off the Iberian Peninsula, frightening a city that was still rebuilding from Lisbon’s catastrophic 1755 quake. The shaking triggered tsunamis and church bells reportedly rang by themselves as the ground swayed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A city shaken twice by devastating quakes fits with an episode about storms, resilience, and the belief that even the most violent weather—literal or emotional—doesn’t last forever.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1931, a powerful earthquake struck Nicaragua and devastated the capital city of Managua, killing around 2,000 people. The disaster triggered fires that raged for days and reshaped the city’s layout as it was rebuilt almost from the ground up.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: After the violence of an earthquake and fire, the idea of a 'Slow Planet' evokes the long, quiet work of rebuilding and the need for a calmer rhythm after sudden catastrophe.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 2023, a historic tornado outbreak tore across the American Midwest and South, spawning dozens of powerful twisters in less than 24 hours. The atmosphere over the region became a vast, churning engine, with supercell storms dropping tornadoes in rapid succession across multiple states.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The explosive energy and violent unpredictability of a tornado outbreak parallels the high-voltage, 'electric shock' feel of this guitar-driven, stormy rock selection.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1931, a Transcontinental & Western Air Fokker F-10 airliner crashed near Bazaar, Kansas, killing all eight people onboard, including legendary Notre Dame football coach Knute Rockne. The tragedy shocked the public and pushed U.S. aviation authorities to tighten safety standards for commercial aircraft.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A tragic early aviation accident contrasts with an episode about a gentle, communal journey at sea, hinting at how far travel safety and the culture of shared journeys have evolved.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1854, U.S. Commodore Matthew Perry signed the Convention of Kanagawa with the Tokugawa Shogunate, forcing the opening of the ports of Shimoda and Hakodate to American trade. This single treaty cracked open Japan’s long policy of isolation and set off a cascade of cultural and technological exchange.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A historic opening of borders and ports connects with a personal story of crossing modern borders to explore Poland, both centered on new routes between cultures and unexpected connections.

published.fact_text: In 1814, the armies of the Sixth Coalition entered Paris after Napoleon’s forces were defeated, bringing the fighting of the Napoleonic Wars to the French capital itself. For Parisians, the fall of their city marked the end of an era that had reshaped Europe through ceaseless war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: The occupation of Paris signaled a forced shift in Europe’s direction; pairing it with an episode about travel, perspective shifts, and moving meditation reframes that upheaval as a moment to reconsider where we are heading.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1933, the United States established the Civilian Conservation Corps, a New Deal program that paid young men to plant trees, build trails, and restore natural landscapes. In less than a decade, the CCC planted billions of trees and helped shape many of the parks and forests people enjoy today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: The CCC’s outdoor work and the creation of recreational landscapes align with an 'Endless Summer' mood of nature, slow days, and living inside the spaces that conservation made possible.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1930, Hollywood studios adopted the Motion Picture Production Code, better known as the Hays Code, a strict set of rules governing how sex, crime, religion, and violence could appear on screen. For nearly four decades, it quietly shaped not just what stories were told, but how entire generations saw morality in moving images.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: A code that censored film images and a technology that 'corrects' music both reveal how industries quietly control what audiences hear and see, making this episode a natural mirror to the Hays Code’s hidden influence.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1901, Antonín Dvořák’s opera "Rusalka" premiered in Prague, telling the eerie, romantic tale of a water nymph who gives up her voice and nature to seek human love. Its haunting "Song to the Moon" would become one of the most beloved arias in the operatic repertoire.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

published.match_reason: Rusalka is a tragic study in irrational, self-sacrificing love; an episode devoted to love’s vulnerable edge and its artistic power is a perfect modern echo of Dvořák’s moonlit opera.
